Jiang et al. High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 It is of great importance to develop specific countermeasures to protect train positioning from jamming-caused GNSS disturbance. Research institutions and GNSS receiver manufacturers have been developing GNSS receivers equipped with anti-jamming capabilities. A survey of state-of-art approaches is introduced, based on the functional stages of the receiver in which they operate. Typically, they could be implemented at the front-end, pre-correction, or post-correlation stages, with others functioning at the navigation processing stage. Spatial fil- tering [9–11], functioning at the front-end stage, uses antenna arrays to point the receiver antenna beam toward the GNSS satellites and away from jammers. In the pre-correction stage, time/frequency filtering [12–14] enables the jamming mitigation before the jamming signal enters the receiver. Vector tracking is a typical approach that works on the post-correction stage. Instead of tracking in-view satellites sepa- rately in the conventional baseband signal processing, vector tracking [15,16] is achieved through feedback of the receiver states from the navigation module to the signal tracking channels as prior information and constraints. Additionally, the inertial-aiding-based strategy at the navigation processing stage provides a different way. INS is a self- contained navigation approach in which measurements provided by accelerometers and gyroscopes are used to track the states relative to a known starting state. Thus, the train maneuver dynamics should be modeled in accordance with the correct train type. An accurate train dynamics model is fundamental to train movement algorithms. 3.1. GNSS testing standard and regulations As illustrated in the introduction part, the public service performance evaluation of GNSS covers two key aspects: SIS performance and system service performance. As the BeiDou Navigation Satellite System Open Service Performance Standard (BDS-OS-PS-3.0) defines, the BDS service performance should cover 5 core indicators: positioning accuracy, velocity measurement accuracy, timing accuracy, PDOP availability, and positioning service availability, as shown in Fig. 4. In terms of positioning accuracy, the specification clarifies the statistical standard of geographical location mea- surement error and specifies the allowable error range under different geographical areas and environmental conditions. The evaluation of speed measurement accuracy is concerned with the accuracy of the user equip- ment to measure the motion speed, which is used in dynamic scenarios, such as transportation and railway applications. Timing accuracy is aimed at high-precision time synchronization services, which are critical cap- abilities in fields such as communications, energy, and scientific experi- ments. The availability of PDOP is an important index to evaluate the effect of geometric configuration on positioning accuracy and to measure the degree of support of satellite layout to the stability of positioning solution. Location service availability measures the ability to provide continuous and reliable service at a given time and area from the perspective of the overall functionality of the system. In addition, the standard also specifies the measurement methods of this performance, including the evaluation environment, data collection method, calculation method, and evaluation cycle specific requirements. Um diesen Herausforderungen zu begegnen, stellen Digitale Zwillinge ein Konzept zur ganzheitlichen Informations- bereitstellung und -nutzung dar. Allerdings wird der Begriff des Digitalen Zwillings sowohl in der Praxis als auch in der wissenschaftlichen Literatur nicht einheitlich verwendet – allein in der wissenschaftlichen Literatur wurden bislang mehr als 20 Definitionen vorgestellt. Kern der unterschiedlichen Auslegun- gen ist jedoch der Gedanke, dass es sich bei einem Digitalen Zwilling um das virtuelle Abbild eines realen, eindeutig zuordenbaren Objekts handelt. Die Art des Objekts, für das 1 Customer-Relationship-Management 2 Enterprise-Resource-Planning 8 Grundlegende Aspekte des Digitalen Zwillings ein Digitaler Zwilling verwendet werden kann, ist sehr vielfäl- tig. Beispielsweise können Digitale Zwillinge für eine virtuelle Repräsentation von physischen Produkten, Anlagen, Prozessen oder auch Services erstellt werden. Manche Akteure stellen sich einen Digitalen Zwilling als inter- aktives Modell eines Objekts, beispielsweise eines Schiffs oder einer spezifischen Anlage, vor. Dabei ist bereits an dieser Stelle hervorzuheben, dass ein Digitaler Zwilling weit über z.B. ein rein digitales 3D-Modell des zugehörigen Objekts hinausgeht. Vielmehr ist ein Digitaler Zwilling als eine Sammlung von verschiedenartigen, mit dem Objekt verknüpften Daten sowie zugehörigen Daten- und Prozessmodellen zu ver- stehen. Diese Datensammlung kann ein 3D-Simulationsmodell beinhalten, muss es aber nicht. Ein Digitaler Zwilling erfüllt drei Hauptfunktionen: Bereitstel- lung, Verknüpfung und Nutzung von Informationen� Die Grundfunktion der Informationsbereitstellung beschreibt die Fähigkeit, auf so viele relevante Informationen wie nötig über das zugehörige reale Objekt zugreifen zu können. Evans / Filsfils (2007) stellen wie in Kapitel 1 angedeutet die Frage, ob die Kosten des Einsatzes von DiffServ (Priorisierung) die Vorteile rechtfertigen.62 Die Vorteile einer DiffServ Implementierung können entweder daraus bestehen, dass weniger Kapazität nötig ist, um die gleiche Verkehrsqualität zu erreichen, oder dass über die gleiche Ka- pazität mehr aggregierter Verkehr transportiert werden kann. Dies hängt vom konkreten Ausbau und den relativen Verkehrsanteilen ab. Dabei kann gezeigt werden, dass die relativen Vorteile dann besonders hoch sind, wenn a) das Verhältnis von anspruchsvol- lem zu weniger anspruchsvollem Verkehr klein ist (vgl. Abbildung 3-8), und wenn b) der Überdimensionierungsfaktor des anspruchsvollen Verkehrs im Verhältnis zum weniger anspruchsvollen Verkehr groß ist.63 Im linken Fall von Abbildung 3-8 hat ein sehr hoher Teil des Gesamtverkehrs sensible Anforderungen an Echtzeit. In diesem Fall können durch eine Priorisierung kaum Vor- teile erzeugt werden, weil es nur wenig Best-Effort Verkehr gibt, auf dessen Kosten Echtzeitverkehr priorisiert behandelt werden könnte. In diesem Fall wird die Netzdimen- sionierung erheblich höher ausfallen als im rechten Fall, wo durch Priorisierung leichter die QoS der Echtzeitdienste gewährleistet werden kann und folglich weniger Netzkapa- zität insgesamt vorgehalten werden muss. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning
content.abstract_or_snippet: is a self- contained navigation approach in which measurements provided by accelerometers and gyroscopes are used to track the states relative to a known starting state. A remarkable advantage of an INS is that it is inherently immune to Radio Frequency (RF) signals, which makes it possible to benefit from the mitigation of GNSS jamming signals. In the railway domain, an extensive analysis of interference events based on a one-year dataset collected across the Swiss railway network was con- ducted in Ref. [8], which revealed widespread occurrences of GNSS interference, particularly clustered around larger cities and densely populated residential areas. A test and evaluation of GNSS-based railway positioning under various jamming conditions was presented in Ref. [17], demonstrating the susceptibility of train localization perfor- mance to interference. A resilient localization framework operating at the measurement level, incorporating jamming discrimination and multi-sensor fusion, was proposed in Ref. [18]. Nevertheless, research on GNSS anti-jamming solutions for railway environments remains re- latively limited, which motivates the focus of the present work. GNSS/INS integration can be categorized into three primary archi- tectures: loosely coupled, tightly coupled, and deeply coupled sys- tems [19,20]. In a loosely coupled integration, the GNSS receiver and INS operate independently, with integration occurring at the navigation solution level by combining the position and velocity outputs from both systems. This approach requires a minimum of four visible satellites for a positioning solution, which can be a limitation in environments with jamming signals. In contrast, tightly-coupled integration integrates raw GNSS measurements, such as pseudoranges and Doppler shifts, directly with INS solution within a filter. This method allows the system to utilize all available satellite signals, even when fewer than four sa- tellites are visible, enhancing performance in challenging environ- ments. Deeply coupled, or ultra-tight, integration goes a step further by incorporating inertial measurements directly into the GNSS signal tracking loops. This integration aids in maintaining signal lock under high dynamics and interference conditions, as the INS can provide predictive information to the GNSS receiver’s tracking algorithms. The advantages of deep integration are particularly evident in environments with significant interference or high dynamics. In Ref. [21], the anti- jamming performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval- uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. While the superiority of INS-aided deep integration has been preliminarily validated in jamming scenarios, current research on anti-j

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning
content.abstract_or_snippet: performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval- uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. While the superiority of INS-aided deep integration has been preliminarily validated in jamming scenarios, current research on anti-jamming methods remains constrained by simple jamming models such as con- tinuous wave jamming. The mechanisms under different jamming modulation schemes remain underexplored. This study expands the exploration of jamming types by focusing on sweep-frequency inter- ference. Considering the compatibility of the GNSS/INS deeply-coupled integration with the current GNSS-based train positioning function in the control system conception, this paper proposes an INS-aided train determination system (LDS) based on the deep integration. The system profile and the GNSS jamming signals are briefly introduced firstly. Then, we give a detailed introduction of deeply-coupled integration design. Finally, a hardware simulator is used and semi-physical ex- periments are conducted to assess the proposed system’s performance with a sweep frequency jamming scenario. 2. System design From Fig. 1, it can be found that multiple information resources are involved in the train positioning system, where the inertial data from the accelerometers and gyroscopes is taken into the Position, Velocity, and Timing (PVT) calculation to enhance the resilience to abnormal jamming attacks. By integrating an INS directly into the train onboard subsystem, it becomes unnecessary to install or modify the track-side subsystem, thereby simplifying system design and maintenance, thereby providing a flexible architecture for modern rail train control systems. From a signal processing perspective, jamming signals are collected and analyzed together with the authentic GNSS signals by the antenna and the subsequent front-end stage in the GNSS receiver simulta- neously. The signal contaminated by jamming signals would directly affect the subsequent stages of the receiver’s processing chain, in- cluding acquisition and tracking stages. In the tracking stage, jamming prevents the code and carrier loops from maintaining stable signal tracking, ultimately resulting in pronounced errors or even failure in the PVT solution. In the proposed system, the Doppler shift information derived from INS is fed back to aid the GNSS tracking loop, improving the overall resilience of the navigation solution. Inertial mechanization is used to infer Inertial Solution at discrete epochs, while the GNSS tracking loop operates simultaneously until they meet the integration epoch. Then, integrated filter realizes the state estimation based on the Fig. 1. Architecture of the GNSS/INS-int

Based on this traditional tightly-coupled GNSS/INS integra- tion, the estimated navigation states are used to estimate the Doppler shift information, which is fed back to the GNSS tracking loop to aid the tracking process. The system operates in a closed-loop way where the integrated filter continuously updates the system’s state estimation by combining data from both the GNSS and INS, and the feedback of the estimated states helps improve the accuracy of both the INS and GNSS calculations. The INS-aided deep integration system leverages the in- ertial data, effectively narrowing the bandwidth of the tracking loop, which helps reduce the noise within the loop and suppress external jamming signals. The system’s capability to maintain accurate tracking and position estimation in jamming environments is significantly strengthened, leading to a more resilient navigation solution. 3. Jamming analysis and protection 3.1. Jamming analysis The received signal strength of the GNSS receiver is extremely low and falls well below the thermal noise floor. Typically, the received GPS signal levels fall within the range of −127 dBm [22]. The weak char- acteristics of GNSS signals will lead to an increasing risk of performance degradation under jamming conditions. Jamming signals may emanate from telecommunication and electronic systems operating in adjacent bands or in bands relatively far from the GNSS bands, i.e., Fre- quency Modulation (FM)/TV transmitter harmonics, Amplitude Mod- ulation (AM) transmitters, and mobile phone networks. In the simulated test, a GNSS signal at GPS L1 frequency is simulated with a reference level of −130 dBm, while the jamming generator operates at a center frequency of 1575.42 MHz with a power level of −90 dBm. The Jamming-to-Signal Ratio (JSR), i.e., the ratio of the jamming signal power to the GNSS signal power, is used to express the relationship between GNSS signal power and jamming power. Beyond complete signal obstruction scenarios, intermediate jamming power levels can induce significant performance degradation in GNSS re- ceivers without fully blocking satellite signal acquisition. These inter- mediate power levels would be hazardous and difficult to detect and mitigate, making it crucial to accurately assess the impact of jamming on GNSS signal quality. Previous research [18] has demonstrated sub- stantial signal quality degradation under jamming conditions with JSR values ranging from 38 to 54 dB. Accordingly, this study focuses on a 40 dB JSR scenario, corresponding to the specified −130 dBm GPS signal and −90 dBm jamming power configuration. A 5 Hz ramp waveform is employed, sweeping ± 1 kHz around the carrier. The jamming signal for investigating the collected IF signal, and the software-defined radio GNSS receiver are used to analyze the data. This software receiver is configured with a second-order phase-locked loop and a second-order code delay lock loop. [23–25], based on the time-frequency behavior, jamming signals can be typically divided into several classes, including Continuous Wave (CW), AM, FM, Frequency Sweep (FS; also referred to is the sampling interval, and as chirp jamming), Narrow-Band Gaussian white Noise (NBGN), and Wideband Gaussian white Noise (WBGN). It is found in Ref. [25] that the majority of the jammers use chirp signals, which vary the signal frequency linearly over time . The chirp signal exhibits a constant rate of frequency change over time, within a certain frequency range and sweep period. This sweep procedure is characterized by the initial and frequency , and the sweep period T. Thus, the instantaneous frequency is , the minimum and maximum frequency at given by The time-domain representation of the chirp signal is modeled as (2) (3) The jamming signals will not only disrupt the proper functioning of the receiver’s RF front-end components, such as low-noise amplifiers, automatic gain control, and analog-to-digital converters, but also ad- versely affect the operation of the carrier and code tracking loops. This jamming can prevent the receiver from acquiring and maintaining a stable lock on the satellite signals, ultimately leading to degraded po- sitioning performance. 3.2. INS-aided jamming protection solution Fig. 2 illustrates the INS-aided GNSS tracking loop. The carrier signal measurement enters the tracking loop through the phase dis- criminator to generate a phase error estimate. This phase error drives the carrier loop filter which outputs an estimate of the signal Doppler to drive the carrier controller oscillator (NCO). The NCO duplicates the phase of the incoming signal, and this phase estimate is fed back to the phase discriminator. The tracking loop is usually said to be “locked” if the phase error is less than five degrees. The setup of the GNSS jamming test platform is shown in Fig. 4, consisting of several key components, each connected through specific signal transmission paths, designed to enable jamming data collection for analytical processing. The Spirent GSS8000 GNSS simulator and the sight N5172B jamming signal generator are con- trolled from the simulator’s SimGEN host controller, which generates the required GPS L1 RF signals and the jamming signals, respectively. The jamming signals are defined, along with all the other scenario parameters, within the SimGEN. The scenario trajectory collected in the field is input into the SimGEN software, driving the simulator to generate the corre- sponding dynamic GNSS RF signals. A signal combiner is utilized to in- tegrate the clean GPS RF signal and the specific jamming signal. Since the down-converted Intermediate Frequency (IF) signal is what we need, the HG-softGPS06 GNSS IF signal collector is connected to the simulated signal to capture the necessary data, which is then collected and post-processed by the PC host. Field test results from a train location system were collected and used as the scenario script to drive the controller. A tactical-grade SPAN system (a high-precision GNSS/INS integrated system with a tactical grade, low noise IMU-FSAS) installed on the train was utilized to collect the tra- jectory and the IMU data. Two practical local trajectories that last for 160 s are adopted as the operation script to generate different GNSS jamming tested scenarios and drive the GNSS simulator. Overall, these results highlight the effectiveness of INS-aided solution in improving the accuracy and stability of both positioning and velocity estimation. The integration of inertial sensors helps to minimize errors caused by jamming, thereby enhancing the overall performance of the naviga- tion system. 191 Z. Cao, J. Liu, W. Jiang et al. High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 Fig. 8. The positioning errors and the velocity errors for the normal tracking solution and the INS-aided solution in scenario II. 5. Conclusion In this paper, a GNSS jamming protection solution is presented based on the INS-aided deep integration solution. The solution enables a self-contained PVT reference that is immune to RF signals. INS in- formation is sent to the receiver’s baseband, realizing the assistance to the receiver at the signal processing level. The measured velocity in- formation from INS is mapped to the LOS between the satellite and the vehicle to obtain the Doppler of relative motion. The Doppler is fed back to the NCO in the tracking loop to aid the tracking process. The the proposed solution demonstrates significant evaluation of improvements in positioning and velocity, enabling resilient train po- sitioning for securing next generation GNSS-based train control sys- tems. Future work will focus on refining mitigation techniques with extended studies for multi-constellation scenarios (e.g., BeiDou and Galileo), and exploring field tests in real lines to ensure the resilience of GNSS-based train localization systems in railway applications. content.abstract_or_snippet: will be integrated to achieve the required accuracy level, reduce the uncertainty and en- hance the dependability. 2.1. GNSS/INS/Odometer sensor data fusion The ODO (Odometer) is the most commonly used train positioning method in existing train control systems. Wheel slide and slip during the train operation, as well as wheel diameter wear, may result in errors in the train speed measurement, and lead to error accumulation in the along-track location calculation results. Such errors will be brought into the INS/ODO integrated positioning system. The train positioning error by INS/ODO drifts over time until the position calibration results are available. Different from the Balises, results of GNSS positioning can provide the correction without relying on the track-side. Performance of a GNSS/INS/ODO integrated positioning system will be determined by the reliability and accuracy of GNSS. When the GNSS performance decreases or the GNSS signal is blocked for a long time, the result of INS will not be guaranteed effectively [6]. Therefore, the availability and observation quality of GNSS greatly affect the precision and effective- ness of train positioning using INS and ODO. The key issue in realizing the multi-sensor integrated train positioning is the sensor fusion logic. It is in charge of combining the measurements from multiple sensors, improving the reliability and fault tolerance of the integrated system, providing suitable auxiliary information for the ex- isting location determination system, and simplifying the system com- plexity over those track-side-relied solutions. Among different sensor fu- sion algorithms, Federated Kalman Filter (FKF) is a distributed cascaded integrated sensor fusion method and widely considered in applications because of its flexibility, low computational effort, and excellent fault tolerance capability. FKF consists of several local filters and a main filter, in which the system information is allocated in the local filters and then fused in the main filter. For the GNSS/INS/ODO integrated train posi- tioning system based on the FKF, INS is integrated due to its advantages in high output rate, autonomy and reliability. Considering the INS-related local filtering structures, two local filters for GNSS/INS and INS/ODO can 48 Fig. 1. Architecture of GNSS/INS/ODO sensor data fusion for integrated train positioning. be established, as shown in Fig. 1. content.abstract_or_snippet: graph optimization. The system tightly couples the raw observation data of LiDAR, vision and IMU, while adding the positioning information of GNSS as a loosely coupled component to reduce the cumulative error. They use factor graph optimization methods to integrate data from different sensors and improve the accuracy and reliability of positioning through nonlinear optimization. In order to solve the limitations of SLAM algorithms in map drift, the literature [158] proposed a sensor fusion SLAM and positioning method based on LiDAR for offline mapping and online positioning of autonomous driving vehicles, integrating LiDAR with other sensors such as GNSS, IMU and vehicle status data to improve map accuracy and positioning performance, even when satellite signals are unavailable or unreliable. This type of fusion will have many advantages by combining the power of accurate LiDAR depth estimation and camera tracking capabilities. However, the fusion of LIDAR and visual SLAM will produce large cumulative errors in high- speed motion. Therefore, low-cost and high-performance inertial sensing units have become the first choice to make up for this defect [159]. Reference [160] proposed the first LiDAR–inertial–vision fusion SLAM system, LVI-SLAM. The system uses a tightly coupled framework to fuse the measurements of all three sensors, addressing the challenges of noisy point clouds, fast sensor movement, and tunnel environments. Reference [161] proposed mVIL-Fusion, a new SLAM system designed to fuse data from a monocular camera, an IMU (inertial measurement unit), and a 3D rotating LiDAR. The system aims to overcome the limitations of traditional SLAM methods, such as time synchronization issues and poor mapping performance in highly variable environments. Reference [162] proposed an effective positioning algorithm for autonomous vehicles based on vision–LiDAR–IMU fusion. In order to solve the problem of excessive back-end optimization calculations, a balanced selection strategy is adopted to reduce the computational complexity, focusing on key frames and sliding windows to optimize pose estimation. In dealing with large- scale drift, a loop detection algorithm based on iterative closest point (ICP) is proposed to improve long-term positioning accuracy. High-speed Railway 1 (2023) 47–55 and the Doppler radar, also show great potentials in realizing the au- tonomous train positioning with a low cost over the Balise-based solu- tions [4]. However, it can be found that the single-sensor-based solution cannot satisfy the requirement of the train control system due to specific limitations and disadvantages in practical scenarios [5]. How to explore the capabilities of multiple perception sensors and break through the application-demand-oriented information fusion processing technology is the key to realize the satisfaction of novel train control systems with the train-centric design. Hence, it is important to overcome the key technologies of autonomous train positioning based on the fusion of GNSS and assistant sensors. By developing a novel train control system with dynamic configuration of the trains’ interval, reduced involvement of track-side equipments, and enhanced maintainability of the on-board train positioning system, the advantages of the train-centric mode will be practically reflected and utilized for specific railway lines. In this paper, key technologies of the novel train control system with GNSS- based autonomous perception are investigated and demonstrated in the practical environment, which illustrate the necessity and advantages of the utilization of GNSS, especially BDS (BeiDou Navigation Satellite System), in future safety-related railway systems in China. 2. Multi-sensor data fusion In existing railway signaling systems, the track occupancy of the train is determined by the track circuit, and the exact along-track lo- cation of the train is calculated by integrating both the train-borne odometer (or Doppler radar) and track-side Balises. The novel train- centric signaling system determines the track occupancy and train lo- cation without relying on those track-side facilities. Thus, raw mea- surements from multiple train-borne sensors will be integrated to achieve the required accuracy level, reduce the uncertainty and en- hance the dependability. 2.1. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Zero on-site testing on GNSS for train control towards digital railways
content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 116–124 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article , Wenzheng Qiaoa, Baigen Caia,b,c, Jian Wanga,b,c, Jiang Liua,b,c, Yuchen Zhaoa, Zero on-site testing on GNSS for train control towards digital railways Debiao Lua,b,c,⁎ Chunjie Qiaod, Dirk Spiegele a School of Automation and Intelligence, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c Beijing Engineering Center for EMC and GNSS Technologies, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China d Hunan Matrix Electronic Technology Co., Ltd., Changsha 410205, China e DB InfraGO AG, Berlin 10557, Germany ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: GNSS testing Railway train localization 3D environment modelling Digital track map Zero on-site testing The integration of Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) technology into railway train control systems is a crucial step toward achieving the vision of a digital railway. Traditional train control systems undergo extensive in-house tests and prolonged field tests for certification and approval before operational deployment, leading to high costs, delays, and operational disruptions. This paper introduces a GNSS-based train control localization framework which eliminates the need for on-site testing by leveraging train movement dynamics and 3D en- vironment modeling to create a zero on-site testing platform. The proposed framework simulates train move- ment and the surrounding 3D environment using collected railway line location data and environmental attri- butes to generate realistic multipath signals and obscuration effects. This approach enables comprehensive laboratory-based case studies for train localization, reducing the huge amount test of needed for physical field trials. The framework is established in house, using the data collected at the Test Base of China Academy of Railway Sciences (Circular Railway). Results from the open area and cutting environment tests demonstrate high localization accuracy repeatability within the simulated environment, validating the feasibility and effectiveness of zero on-site testing for GNSS-based train control systems. This research highlights the potential of GNSS simulation platforms in enhancing cost efficiency, operational safety, and accuracy for future digital railways.

Conclusion This paper introduces a zero on-site testing platform on GNSS for train control towards digital railways, the platform utilizes the GNSS RF signal to be broadcasted through the multiplier of both GNSS signal and multipath signal, which enables the receiver to operate in an environ- ment where the signal reception is identical to the physical world. The proposed methods in this paper train dynamic modeling, 3D environ- ment modeling with digital track map, and the ray-tracing techniques. With the proposed method and the setup of the zero on-site platform, the tests on open area and cutting environment are tested, and the test results demonstrate the zero on-site ability of the platform. Further, repeatability and reproducibility need to be performed for the accreditation of the platform for the adoption of the certification process for the GNSS in railway applications. This research highlights the potential of GNSS simulation platforms in enhancing cost efficiency, operational safety, and accuracy for future digital railways. 1. Introduction Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSSs) have seen vast applications in the transportation domain. The integration of Global Positioning System (GPS) and Beidou Navigation Satellite System (BDS) in railway is efficient for modernizing the railway network, GNSS is increasingly becoming a fundamental pillar not only for non-safety applications, but also for im- proved maintenance, automated train operation, and safety- related appli- cations, including the Next Generation of Train Control (NGTC) and re- novation of the train on-board equipment, all these will increase the railway system capacity, efficiency and safety of all railways [1]. To apply GNSS in railway train operation, the GNSS performance testing and verification is the essential procedure for the system to be adopted and further listed as mandatory in the system design and development. The certification of the GNSS-based train localization systems is also necessary to provide concrete validation or testing application services to carry out simulation tests with evaluation of the platform and environmental condi- tion simulations [2]. Beidou has a set of technical and regulatory standards, including the BDS Performance Standard, BDS Signal Interface Control Document (ICD), and technical requirements for user equipment. The BDS Signal In Space (SIS) and service performance characteristics include ac- curacy, availability, compatibility, and interoperability [3]. GPS has also published standard positioning service performance standards to guide the user for civil uses worldwide, the performance metrics are GPS SIS accuracy, continuity, and availability [4]. So both BDS and GPS are focusing on ac- curacy as the foundation for the GNSS performance [5]. The performance needs to be assessed and as the performances are all credited to the equipment, the equipment itself needs to be certified. The certification procedure, it is governed by various international and regional standards, such as the accuracy standards by BDS and GPS, the test and simulation standard procedures, the simulation must meet possible (as sensible to the GNSS) to make the test results plausible. Liso Nicolás [13] used ray tracing to describe the channel characteristics between the satellite and the ground receiver and ex- tracted information from it to design a more positive algorithm, redu- cing the influence of multipath effects on positioning. This paper introduces a zero on-site testing scheme for GNSS perfor- mance evaluation for railway train control system adoption. The paper is organized as follows. Section 2 introduces the problems and challenges in the methods for zero on-site testing, Section 3 introduces the key technol- ogies in the testing procedure. After that, Section 4 introduces the data collected using the zero on-site testing platform, and Section 5 delivers the conclusion of the testing performance and characteristics. 2) GNSS signal propagation: with the train movement, the GNSS signal propagation in the real world can be affected by the environment along the railway track. As the optimized signal to be propagated is the Line-Of-Sight (LOS) signal, due to the obstacles along the track, the GNSS signal might be reflected, refracted, and blocked. Thus, environmental modelling is the basis for simulation. There are still many challenges in addressing scientific problems. The main problems to be discussed in this paper is the environmental modelling forming the ray-tracing procedure, and the train movement that integrates the environment and train location to generate the ap- propriate GNSS signal for the zero on-site testing. 2. The zero on-site testing procedure shall be composed of the procedure starting with the test control and management, then the train movement and simulation. content.title: Study on key technologies of GNSS-based train state perception for train-centric railway signaling
content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 1 (2023) 47–55 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article Study on key technologies of GNSS-based train state perception for train- centric railway signaling Baigen Caia,b,⁎ a School of Computer and Information Technology, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c School of Geodesy and Geomatics, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430079, China d GNSS Research Center, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430079, China e Space Engineering University, Beijing 101416, China , Jingnan Liuc,d, Xurong Donge, Jiang Liub ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: Railway signaling Train control Global Navigation Satellite System Sensor data fusion Virtual Balise Train integrity Performance evaluation The application of Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSSs) in the intelligent railway systems is rapidly developing all over the world. With the GNSS-based train positioning and moving state perception, the au- tonomy and flexibility of a novel train control system can be greatly enhanced over the existing solutions relying on the track-side facilities. Considering the safety critical features of the railway signaling applications, the GNSS stand-alone mode may not be sufficient to satisfy the practical requirements. In this paper, the key technologies for applying GNSS in novel train-centric railway signaling systems are investigated, including the multi-sensor data fusion, Virtual Balise (VB) capturing and messaging, train integrity monitoring and system performance evaluation. According to the practical characteristics of the novel train control system under the moving block mode, the details of the key technologies are introduced. Field demonstration results of a novel train control system using the presented technologies under the practical railway operation conditions are presented to il- lustrate the achievable performance feature of autonomous train state perception using BeiDou Navigation Satellite System (BDS) and related solutions. It reveals the great potentials of these key technologies in the next generation train control system and other GNSS-based railway implementations. 1. Introduction The train control system plays a significant role in ensuring the safety of railway operation and improving the transportation efficiency. Chinese Train Control Systems (CTCSs), including CTCS level 2 and level 3, have been

Heterogeneous sensor data fusion can eliminate information redundancy and loss, and provide reliable, stable, and efficient environmental perception capabilities, but the cost of the system will increase accordingly. 2.4. Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)/Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) The Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) and Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) are key components in autonomous driving systems, helping to determine the position, speed, and orientation of the vehicle. GNSS is a satellite-based navigation system that provides precise positioning and timing information anywhere on Earth. By triangulat- ing signals from multiple satellites, a GNSS receiver can calculate the vehicle’s position (latitude, longitude, and altitude) and speed. GNSS is widely used for global positioning and mapping, especially in open areas such as highways. In autonomous driving, it is one of the most commonly used vehicle positioning sensors. GNSS provides precise global positioning, which enables accurate positioning in open environments such as highways or rural roads. Differential GPS (DGPS), Real-Time Kinematic GPS (RTK-GPS), and Precise Point Positioning (PPP) technologies can be used to improve the accuracy of GNSS. The differential global positioning system (DGPS) operation consists of a reference station and a rover. Both stations use GPS receivers to receive positioning data from GPS satellites, use the positioning data collected by the reference station to calculate the positioning error, and transmit the error correction to the rover to improve the positioning accuracy [27]. RTK can achieve real-time centimeter-level positioning accuracy through double-difference ambigu- ity resolution (AR) [28]. Compared with differential techniques, precise point positioning (PPP) has a significant advantage in that it can accurately determine the position of a GNSS rover receiver by using external corrections from the Internet or dedicated correction satellites [29]. While both DGPS and RTK can achieve high positioning accuracy, GPS signal interruptions in urban environments and tunnels remain a challenge for these sensors. The IMU can provide information about the vehicle’s attitude, velocity, and direction. The IMU helps determine vehicle motion between GNSS updates and ensures

content.title: Yang - A Review of Environmental Perception Technology Based on Multi-Sensor Information Fusion in Autonomous Driving
content.abstract_or_snippet: achieve high positioning accuracy, GPS signal interruptions in urban environments and tunnels remain a challenge for these sensors. The IMU can provide information about the vehicle’s attitude, velocity, and direction. The IMU helps determine vehicle motion between GNSS updates and ensures continuous tracking of the vehicle’s path, especially in areas where GNSS signals may be temporarily lost, such as tunnels and dense urban areas. The IMU provides real-time feedback on the ve- hicle’s motion, which is critical for continued tracking, especially in dynamic environments. As the positioning error accumulates during the vehicle’s driving, the IMU signal error accumulation will drift over time, so the IMU needs to continuously correct the estimated position. The data fusion of GNSS and IMU can achieve vehicle state estimation and ensure a continuous positioning process. GNSS and IMU data are often fused with other sensors such as LiDAR and radar to improve positioning accuracy and build a complete picture of the vehicle’s surroundings. By fusing these data sources, autonomous vehicles are able to navigate in a variety of environments, even without direct GNSS signals. There are currently several models for combining GNSS and IMU data. (1) GNSS/IMU integration: combining GNSS data with IMU information can create a dead reckoning system that continuously estimates the vehicle’s position when GNSS signals are unavailable. In this model, the IMU uses accelerometers and gyroscopes to track vehicle motion and orientation to fill in the gaps in GNSS data. (2) Tightly coupled system: in this, GNSS and IMU data are fused and processed in real time. This can improve the accuracy of vehicle positioning, es- pecially in challenging environments where GNSS signals are weak or unstable. (3) Loosely coupled system: in this system, GNSS and IMU operate independently and merge the data in the post-processing stage. Compared with the tightly coupled system, this method is less computationally intensive, but less accurate. World Electr. Veh. J. 2025, 16, 20 8 of 34 To mitigate the limitations of GNSS and IMU, these sensors are often fused with other perception technologies, such as LiDAR, radar, and cameras. GNSS/IMU systems are often integrated with LiDAR and radar sensors to enhance the vehicle’s perception of its surroundings. LiDAR provides a high-precision 3D map of the environment around the vehicle, while radar provides reliable object detection in adverse weather

content.abstract_or_snippet: positioning system should be sufficiently robust and accurate to cope with various complex environments and severe weather conditions. Generally, in autonomous driving systems, it is a common practice to increase the overall positioning and mapping performance by fusing two or more sensors. Reference [152] proposed a camera–radar sensor fusion framework based on vehicle component (rear corner) detection and positioning to improve the stability of vehicle positioning. The main idea of this method is to enhance the azimuth accuracy of radar information by detecting and locating the rear corner of the target vehicle from the image. The proposed method can effectively track the corner points of the vehicle using only one visible corner even in occluded scenes. GPS may experience signal attenuation in indoor spaces and urban canyons, and IMU may drift over time due to error accumulation when integrating acceleration to determine velocity and position. Reference [153] uses an unscented Kalman filter (UKF) Bayesian filter to fuse GPS and IMU data. Reference [154] uses the error state Kalman filter (ESKF) and the piecewise Rauch–Tung–Striebel (RTS) smoothing algorithm to fuse GNSS and IMU to improve the positioning accuracy and robustness of GNSS and IMU sensors. The proposed ESKF-RTS method provides more accurate and reliable positioning for autonomous vehicles, especially in environments where GNSS signals are unreliable. Reference [155] proposes a fusion algorithm that combines the Kalman filter (KF) with a new INS/GPS neural network framework (GI-NN) to assist INS in reducing the accumulated navigation error during GPS signal loss. Reference [156] studies the information fusion of GPS and INS sensors using the Kalman filter. In order to improve the performance of the Kalman filter, a robust method using the Mahalanobis distance is adopted. In order to World Electr. Veh. J. 2025, 16, 20 25 of 34 compensate for the problem of GPS data interruption, an artificial neural network is used to assist GPS\INS information fusion. Visual sensors are important elements in autonomous driving positioning and mapping systems. Reference [157] proposes an improved multi- sensor fusion positioning system based on GNSS/LiDAR/Vision/IMU. With semi-tight coupling and graph optimization. The system tightly couples the raw observation data of LiDAR, vision and IMU, while adding the positioning information of GNSS as a loosely coupled component to reduce the cumulative error. content.title: Study on key technologies of GNSS-based train state perception for train-centric railway signaling
content.abstract_or_snippet: data volume, which releases the require- ment to the train-ground communication and the complexity of system interfaces for trackmap verification. Through the integration of static trackmap information, sensor data fusion for BDS/GPS re- ceivers, INS sensors and the odometer measurements were real- timely achieved with a high availability level under the complex and time-changing operation environment. Both the static and dynamic test cases were involved in the field demonstration. Table 6 sum- marizes the accuracy results of train positioning under different system modes. The results from field demonstration illustrate that involvement of the presented key technologies, including the safe platform and multi- source sensor fusion mechanism, effectively guarantees the perfor- mance of GNSS-based autonomous train positioning and state percep- tion even under complex and time-varying operational conditions. The utilization of the differential GNSS technology and the integrity mon- itoring logic makes it possible of achieving the high accuracy and ro- bustness against the possible difficult or even critical conditions. The field test results demonstrate the feasibility and the advantages of utilizing BDS in realizing a novel autonomous train control system with a train-centric design, which is of great significance in the future high- speed and plateau railway applications with the enhanced environ- mental flexibility and whole life cycle cost efficiency. 7. Conclusion The application of GNSS technology is one of the representative characteristics of intelligent railway systems in the future. However, there are specific requirements to the GNSS-based train positioning and state perception in the railway signaling applications due to the critical re- quirement to the safety. Thus, the utilization of GNSS has to be enhanced by the specific technical solutions. The sensor data fusion technique is of great significance to enhance the service availability under degraded or even a critical GNSS observing condition. To ensure the compatibility to the existing system specifications, the VB technology enables the in- tegration of the GNSS capabilities into the train control system and meanwhile the train integrity monitoring logic allows the opportunity to realize moving block-based train operation control through the dynamic identification of the track occupancy and safe envelope of the train. By investigating the relationship between the GNSS performance system and the RAMS specification, the performance evaluation of the GNSS-based train positioning can be effectively realized. It can be found that the in- volvement of these key technologies consolidates the autonomy and cap- abilities of the train-borne system, which releases the requirement to the track-side facilities like track circuit and Balises. content.title: Yang - A Review of Environmental Perception Technology Based on Multi-Sensor Information Fusion in Autonomous Driving
content.abstract_or_snippet: U systems are often integrated with LiDAR and radar sensors to enhance the vehicle’s perception of its surroundings. LiDAR provides a high-precision 3D map of the environment around the vehicle, while radar provides reliable object detection in adverse weather conditions. The integration of these sensors improves positioning, obstacle detection, and path planning. Cameras are used in conjunction with GNSS/IMUs to help detect road signs, lane markings, and other visual cues that support positioning. By fusing visual data and GNSS/IMU information, autonomous driving systems can achieve higher vehicle positioning and decision-making accuracy. The integration of GNSS and IMU is critical for the precise positioning and navigation of autonomous vehicles. While GNSS provides reliable global positioning services, IMU ensures continuous tracking when GNSS signals are missing or unstable. GNSS/IMU data is fused with sensors such as LiDAR, radar, and cameras to ensure that autonomous vehicles can operate reliably in a variety of environments. In general, the fusion of multi-source heterogeneous sensor data will improve the perception capability and range of autonomous vehicles, but the resulting computational pressure needs to be resolved. The combination of V2V, V2I, and cloud computing will reduce the computational pressure of vehicles processing massive data. 3. Multi-Sensor Information Fusion Autonomous vehicles travel in dynamic and unpredictable environments, and a sin- gle type of sensor cannot provide the comprehensive data required for safe and efficient navigation. Each sensor has its own unique advantages and limitations. Multi-sensor fusion can increase redundancy and reduce the possibility of errors due to sensor-specific limitations. By combining data from multiple sensors, the system obtains overlapping cov- erage, ensuring robust perception while improving the accuracy and reliability of the data. Fusion sensor systems provide more reliable and detailed perception of the environment, which helps make real-time decisions in complex scenarios such as urban navigation and highway driving. By gaining a more comprehensive understanding of the environment, fusion systems can reduce risks by improving object detection, obstacle avoidance, and path planning, enabling autonomous vehicles to adapt to different scenarios. However, the fusion of multiple sensors is also challenging. Since the fusion algorithm requires a lot of computing resources, this increases system complexity and energy consumption. The fusion process requires precise alignment of sensor data in time and space, which is difficult to achieve in real-time applications. Similarly, differences or noise in sensor data can lead to information conflicts, requiring complex algorithms to resolve inconsistencies. hypotheses.statement: GNSS-basierte (on-board) Zugortung kann gegenüber rein streckenseitiger Ortung (z. B. Balisen/Track Circuits) die Abhängigkeit von Trackside-Infrastruktur reduzieren und dadurch Systemdesign und Wartung vereinfachen.
hypotheses.reasoning: Die INS-Integration in ein GNSS-basiertes On-board-System wird explizit als ohne Installation/Modifikation trackseitiger Subsysteme beschrieben und als Vereinfachung von Design und Maintenance bewertet (h5VVQ50BVFQHb0ioz3_D). Train-centric Signaling beschreibt, dass GNSS-basierte Ortung/Belegungsermittlung ohne Trackside-Facilities auskommt und damit Autonomie/Flexibilität gegenüber bestehenden, trackside-basierten Lösungen erhöht (W5VVQ50BVFQHb0ioqH9j; WZVVQ50BVFQHb0ioqH9F).

hypotheses.statement: GNSS liefert als absolute Positionsquelle einen spezifischen Vorteil gegenüber IMU/Odometrie-Dead-Reckoning, weil GNSS (bei ausreichender Signalqualität) Drift- und Akkumulationsfehler von INS/ODO entlang der Strecke kalibrieren/korrigieren kann, ohne auf Balisen angewiesen zu sein.
hypotheses.reasoning: Das Dokument beschreibt, dass Odometer-Fehler (Slip/Slide, Raddurchmesser-Verschleiß) zu Fehlerakkumulation führen und INS/ODO-Positionierung über die Zeit driftet, bis eine Kalibrierung verfügbar ist; GNSS kann diese Korrektur liefern, ohne Trackside-Balisen zu benötigen (XJVVQ50BVFQHb0ioqH9u).

hypotheses.statement: Unter GNSS-Interferenz/Jamming ist GNSS allein im Schienenkontext deutlich anfällig; ein Vorteil gegenüber RF-unabhängigen Sensoren (INS) entsteht primär in hybriden GNSS/INS-Architekturen, insbesondere bei tief gekoppelter (deep/ultra-tight) Integration, die Signal-Tracking und PVT-Resilienz unter Jamming verbessert.
hypotheses.reasoning: Es wird eine einjährige Analyse weit verbreiteter GNSS-Interferenzereignisse im Schweizer Netz sowie die Suszeptibilität GNSS-basierter Lokalisation gegenüber Interferenz referenziert (hpVVQ50BVFQHb0ioz3-5). Deep Integration speist INS-Informationen in GNSS-Tracking-Loops ein und soll Lock/Tracking unter Interferenz stabilisieren (hpVVQ50BVFQHb0ioz3-5; iJVVQ50BVFQHb0ioz3_N). Semi-physische Experimente unter Sweep-Frequency-Jamming berichten signifikante Verbesserungen von Positions- und Geschwindigkeitsfehlern durch INS-aided deep integration gegenüber normalem Tracking (h5VVQ50BVFQHb0ioz3_D; j5VVQ50BVFQHb0io0H8X).

hypotheses.statement: Für GNSS-basierte Zugortung in Train-Control kann eine laborbasierte, simulationsgestützte Test-/Validierungsplattform („zero on-site testing“) gegenüber umfangreichen Feldtests die benötigte Menge an physischen Field Trials reduzieren und damit Kosten, Zeit und operationelle Risiken senken, sofern die Simulation feldtestäquivalente Performance abbildet.
hypotheses.reasoning: Das Paper beschreibt, dass traditionelle Zertifizierung/Approval extensive in-house und prolonged field tests erfordert (Kosten/Verzögerungen/Disruption) und stellt ein Framework vor, das on-site testing eliminieren soll; zugleich wird betont, dass es Field Trials reduziert (SJVVQ50BVFQHb0ioon_7) und dass Field Testing ressourcenintensiv, teuer und risikobehaftet ist (SpVVQ50BVFQHb0ioo38T). Es werden Laborresultate/Repeatability in simulierten Umgebungen berichtet (VJVVQ50BVFQHb0ioo397) sowie die Einordnung, dass Simulation identische Performance wie Field Tests demonstrieren muss und weitere Akkreditierung (Repeatability/Reproducibility) nötig ist (VJVVQ50BVFQHb0ioo397; SZVVQ50BVFQHb0ioo38H).

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning
content.abstract_or_snippet: is a self- contained navigation approach in which measurements provided by accelerometers and gyroscopes are used to track the states relative to a known starting state. A remarkable advantage of an INS is that it is inherently immune to Radio Frequency (RF) signals, which makes it possible to benefit from the mitigation of GNSS jamming signals. In the railway domain, an extensive analysis of interference events based on a one-year dataset collected across the Swiss railway network was con- ducted in Ref. [8], which revealed widespread occurrences of GNSS interference, particularly clustered around larger cities and densely populated residential areas. A test and evaluation of GNSS-based railway positioning under various jamming conditions was presented in Ref. [17], demonstrating the susceptibility of train localization perfor- mance to interference. A resilient localization framework operating at the measurement level, incorporating jamming discrimination and multi-sensor fusion, was proposed in Ref. [18]. Nevertheless, research on GNSS anti-jamming solutions for railway environments remains re- latively limited, which motivates the focus of the present work. GNSS/INS integration can be categorized into three primary archi- tectures: loosely coupled, tightly coupled, and deeply coupled sys- tems [19,20]. In a loosely coupled integration, the GNSS receiver and INS operate independently, with integration occurring at the navigation solution level by combining the position and velocity outputs from both systems. This approach requires a minimum of four visible satellites for a positioning solution, which can be a limitation in environments with jamming signals. In contrast, tightly-coupled integration integrates raw GNSS measurements, such as pseudoranges and Doppler shifts, directly with INS solution within a filter. This method allows the system to utilize all available satellite signals, even when fewer than four sa- tellites are visible, enhancing performance in challenging environ- ments. Deeply coupled, or ultra-tight, integration goes a step further by incorporating inertial measurements directly into the GNSS signal tracking loops. This integration aids in maintaining signal lock under high dynamics and interference conditions, as the INS can provide predictive information to the GNSS receiver’s tracking algorithms. The advantages of deep integration are particularly evident in environments with significant interference or high dynamics. In Ref. [21], the anti- jamming performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval- uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. While the superiority of INS-aided deep integration has been preliminarily validated in jamming scenarios, current research on anti-j

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning
content.abstract_or_snippet: performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval- uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. While the superiority of INS-aided deep integration has been preliminarily validated in jamming scenarios, current research on anti-jamming methods remains constrained by simple jamming models such as con- tinuous wave jamming. The mechanisms under different jamming modulation schemes remain underexplored. This study expands the exploration of jamming types by focusing on sweep-frequency inter- ference. Considering the compatibility of the GNSS/INS deeply-coupled integration with the current GNSS-based train positioning function in the control system conception, this paper proposes an INS-aided train determination system (LDS) based on the deep integration. The system profile and the GNSS jamming signals are briefly introduced firstly. Then, we give a detailed introduction of deeply-coupled integration design. Finally, a hardware simulator is used and semi-physical ex- periments are conducted to assess the proposed system’s performance with a sweep frequency jamming scenario. 2. System design From Fig. 1, it can be found that multiple information resources are involved in the train positioning system, where the inertial data from the accelerometers and gyroscopes is taken into the Position, Velocity, and Timing (PVT) calculation to enhance the resilience to abnormal jamming attacks. By integrating an INS directly into the train onboard subsystem, it becomes unnecessary to install or modify the track-side subsystem, thereby simplifying system design and maintenance, thereby providing a flexible architecture for modern rail train control systems. From a signal processing perspective, jamming signals are collected and analyzed together with the authentic GNSS signals by the antenna and the subsequent front-end stage in the GNSS receiver simulta- neously. The signal contaminated by jamming signals would directly affect the subsequent stages of the receiver’s processing chain, in- cluding acquisition and tracking stages. In the tracking stage, jamming prevents the code and carrier loops from maintaining stable signal tracking, ultimately resulting in pronounced errors or even failure in the PVT solution. In the proposed system, the Doppler shift information derived from INS is fed back to aid the GNSS tracking loop, improving the overall resilience of the navigation solution. Inertial mechanization is used to infer Inertial Solution at discrete epochs, while the GNSS tracking loop operates simultaneously until they meet the integration epoch. High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 information from both the INS and GNSS pseudorange/pseudorange rate, where a specific estimator (e.g., nonlinear Kalman filter) would be adopted. Based on this traditional tightly-coupled GNSS/INS integra- tion, the estimated navigation states are used to estimate the Doppler shift information, which is fed back to the GNSS tracking loop to aid the tracking process. The system operates in a closed-loop way where the integrated filter continuously updates the system’s state estimation by combining data from both the GNSS and INS, and the feedback of the estimated states helps improve the accuracy of both the INS and GNSS calculations. The INS-aided deep integration system leverages the in- ertial data, effectively narrowing the bandwidth of the tracking loop, which helps reduce the noise within the loop and suppress external jamming signals. The system’s capability to maintain accurate tracking and position estimation in jamming environments is significantly strengthened, leading to a more resilient navigation solution. 3. Jamming analysis and protection 3.1. Jamming analysis The received signal strength of the GNSS receiver is extremely low and falls well below the thermal noise floor. Typically, the received GPS signal levels fall within the range of −127 dBm [22]. The weak char- acteristics of GNSS signals will lead to an increasing risk of performance degradation under jamming conditions. Jamming signals may emanate from telecommunication and electronic systems operating in adjacent bands or in bands relatively far from the GNSS bands, i.e., Fre- quency Modulation (FM)/TV transmitter harmonics, Amplitude Mod- ulation (AM) transmitters, and mobile phone networks. Scenario II: The trajectory starts at GPS week 2034, second of week 289800, lasting for 160 s. The trajectory also replicated a train journey along the Qinghai–Xizang Railway from Nagqu to Amdo section. A number of in-view satellites is up to 5. The velocity continuously ac- celerated from 25 to 27 m/s. In the simulated test, a GNSS signal at GPS L1 frequency is simulated with a reference level of −130 dBm, while the jamming generator operates at a center frequency of 1575.42 MHz with a power level of −90 dBm. The Jamming-to-Signal Ratio (JSR), i.e., the ratio of the jamming signal power to the GNSS signal power, is used to express the relationship between GNSS signal power and jamming power. Beyond complete signal obstruction scenarios, intermediate jamming power levels can induce significant performance degradation in GNSS re- ceivers without fully blocking satellite signal acquisition. These inter- mediate power levels would be hazardous and difficult to detect and mitigate, making it crucial to accurately assess the impact of jamming on GNSS signal quality. Previous research [18] has demonstrated sub- stantial signal quality degradation under jamming conditions with JSR values ranging from 38 to 54 dB. Accordingly, this study focuses on a 40 dB JSR scenario, corresponding to the specified −130 dBm GPS signal and −90 dBm jamming power configuration. A 5 Hz ramp waveform is employed, sweeping ± 1 kHz around the carrier. The jamming signal for investigating the collected IF signal, and the software-defined radio GNSS receiver are used to analyze the data. This software receiver is configured with a second-order phase-locked loop and a second-order code delay lock loop. [23–25], based on the time-frequency behavior, jamming signals can be typically divided into several classes, including Continuous Wave (CW), AM, FM, Frequency Sweep (FS; also referred to is the sampling interval, and as chirp jamming), Narrow-Band Gaussian white Noise (NBGN), and Wideband Gaussian white Noise (WBGN). It is found in Ref. [25] that the majority of the jammers use chirp signals, which vary the signal frequency linearly over time . The chirp signal exhibits a constant rate of frequency change over time, within a certain frequency range and sweep period. This sweep procedure is characterized by the initial and frequency , and the sweep period T. Thus, the instantaneous frequency is , the minimum and maximum frequency at given by The time-domain representation of the chirp signal is modeled as (2) (3) The jamming signals will not only disrupt the proper functioning of the receiver’s RF front-end components, such as low-noise amplifiers, automatic gain control, and analog-to-digital converters, but also ad- versely affect the operation of the carrier and code tracking loops. This jamming can prevent the receiver from acquiring and maintaining a stable lock on the satellite signals, ultimately leading to degraded po- sitioning performance. 3.2. INS-aided jamming protection solution Fig. 2 illustrates the INS-aided GNSS tracking loop. The carrier signal measurement enters the tracking loop through the phase dis- criminator to generate a phase error estimate. This phase error drives the carrier loop filter which outputs an estimate of the signal Doppler to drive the carrier controller oscillator (NCO). The NCO duplicates the phase of the incoming signal, and this phase estimate is fed back to the phase discriminator. The tracking loop is usually said to be “locked” if the phase error is less than five degrees. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning
content.abstract_or_snippet: a signal jamming test environment. The setup of the GNSS jamming test platform is shown in Fig. 4, consisting of several key components, each connected through specific signal transmission paths, designed to enable jamming data collection for analytical processing. The Spirent GSS8000 GNSS simulator and the sight N5172B jamming signal generator are con- trolled from the simulator’s SimGEN host controller, which generates the required GPS L1 RF signals and the jamming signals, respectively. The jamming signals are defined, along with all the other scenario parameters, within the SimGEN. The scenario trajectory collected in the field is input into the SimGEN software, driving the simulator to generate the corre- sponding dynamic GNSS RF signals. A signal combiner is utilized to in- tegrate the clean GPS RF signal and the specific jamming signal. Since the down-converted Intermediate Frequency (IF) signal is what we need, the HG-softGPS06 GNSS IF signal collector is connected to the simulated signal to capture the necessary data, which is then collected and post-processed by the PC host. Field test results from a train location system were collected and used as the scenario script to drive the controller. A tactical-grade SPAN system (a high-precision GNSS/INS integrated system with a tactical grade, low noise IMU-FSAS) installed on the train was utilized to collect the tra- jectory and the IMU data. Two practical local trajectories that last for 160 s are adopted as the operation script to generate different GNSS jamming tested scenarios and drive the GNSS simulator. The key parameters of the trajectory are listed as follows. Scenario I: The trajectory starts at GPS week 2034, second of week 288888, lasting for 160 s. content.abstract_or_snippet: solution being 0.12 m/s. Overall, these results highlight the effectiveness of INS-aided solution in improving the accuracy and stability of both positioning and velocity estimation. The integration of inertial sensors helps to minimize errors caused by jamming, thereby enhancing the overall performance of the naviga- tion system. 191 Z. Cao, J. Liu, W. Jiang et al. High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 Fig. 8. The positioning errors and the velocity errors for the normal tracking solution and the INS-aided solution in scenario II. 5. Conclusion In this paper, a GNSS jamming protection solution is presented based on the INS-aided deep integration solution. The solution enables a self-contained PVT reference that is immune to RF signals. INS in- formation is sent to the receiver’s baseband, realizing the assistance to the receiver at the signal processing level. The measured velocity in- formation from INS is mapped to the LOS between the satellite and the vehicle to obtain the Doppler of relative motion. The Doppler is fed back to the NCO in the tracking loop to aid the tracking process. The the proposed solution demonstrates significant evaluation of improvements in positioning and velocity, enabling resilient train po- sitioning for securing next generation GNSS-based train control sys- tems. Future work will focus on refining mitigation techniques with extended studies for multi-constellation scenarios (e.g., BeiDou and Galileo), and exploring field tests in real lines to ensure the resilience of GNSS-based train localization systems in railway applications. GNSS/INS/Odometer sensor data fusion The ODO (Odometer) is the most commonly used train positioning method in existing train control systems. Wheel slide and slip during the train operation, as well as wheel diameter wear, may result in errors in the train speed measurement, and lead to error accumulation in the along-track location calculation results. Such errors will be brought into the INS/ODO integrated positioning system. The train positioning error by INS/ODO drifts over time until the position calibration results are available. Different from the Balises, results of GNSS positioning can provide the correction without relying on the track-side. Performance of a GNSS/INS/ODO integrated positioning system will be determined by the reliability and accuracy of GNSS. When the GNSS performance decreases or the GNSS signal is blocked for a long time, the result of INS will not be guaranteed effectively [6]. Therefore, the availability and observation quality of GNSS greatly affect the precision and effective- ness of train positioning using INS and ODO. The key issue in realizing the multi-sensor integrated train positioning is the sensor fusion logic. It is in charge of combining the measurements from multiple sensors, improving the reliability and fault tolerance of the integrated system, providing suitable auxiliary information for the ex- isting location determination system, and simplifying the system com- plexity over those track-side-relied solutions. Among different sensor fu- sion algorithms, Federated Kalman Filter (FKF) is a distributed cascaded integrated sensor fusion method and widely considered in applications because of its flexibility, low computational effort, and excellent fault tolerance capability. FKF consists of several local filters and a main filter, in which the system information is allocated in the local filters and then fused in the main filter. For the GNSS/INS/ODO integrated train posi- tioning system based on the FKF, INS is integrated due to its advantages in high output rate, autonomy and reliability. Considering the INS-related local filtering structures, two local filters for GNSS/INS and INS/ODO can 48 Fig. 1. Architecture of GNSS/INS/ODO sensor data fusion for integrated train positioning. be established, as shown in Fig. 1. The system tightly couples the raw observation data of LiDAR, vision and IMU, while adding the positioning information of GNSS as a loosely coupled component to reduce the cumulative error. They use factor graph optimization methods to integrate data from different sensors and improve the accuracy and reliability of positioning through nonlinear optimization. In order to solve the limitations of SLAM algorithms in map drift, the literature [158] proposed a sensor fusion SLAM and positioning method based on LiDAR for offline mapping and online positioning of autonomous driving vehicles, integrating LiDAR with other sensors such as GNSS, IMU and vehicle status data to improve map accuracy and positioning performance, even when satellite signals are unavailable or unreliable. This type of fusion will have many advantages by combining the power of accurate LiDAR depth estimation and camera tracking capabilities. However, the fusion of LIDAR and visual SLAM will produce large cumulative errors in high- speed motion. Therefore, low-cost and high-performance inertial sensing units have become the first choice to make up for this defect [159]. Reference [160] proposed the first LiDAR–inertial–vision fusion SLAM system, LVI-SLAM. The system uses a tightly coupled framework to fuse the measurements of all three sensors, addressing the challenges of noisy point clouds, fast sensor movement, and tunnel environments. Reference [161] proposed mVIL-Fusion, a new SLAM system designed to fuse data from a monocular camera, an IMU (inertial measurement unit), and a 3D rotating LiDAR. The system aims to overcome the limitations of traditional SLAM methods, such as time synchronization issues and poor mapping performance in highly variable environments. Reference [162] proposed an effective positioning algorithm for autonomous vehicles based on vision–LiDAR–IMU fusion. In order to solve the problem of excessive back-end optimization calculations, a balanced selection strategy is adopted to reduce the computational complexity, focusing on key frames and sliding windows to optimize pose estimation. In dealing with large- scale drift, a loop detection algorithm based on iterative closest point (ICP) is proposed to improve long-term positioning accuracy. However, it can be found that the single-sensor-based solution cannot satisfy the requirement of the train control system due to specific limitations and disadvantages in practical scenarios [5]. How to explore the capabilities of multiple perception sensors and break through the application-demand-oriented information fusion processing technology is the key to realize the satisfaction of novel train control systems with the train-centric design. Hence, it is important to overcome the key technologies of autonomous train positioning based on the fusion of GNSS and assistant sensors. By developing a novel train control system with dynamic configuration of the trains’ interval, reduced involvement of track-side equipments, and enhanced maintainability of the on-board train positioning system, the advantages of the train-centric mode will be practically reflected and utilized for specific railway lines. In this paper, key technologies of the novel train control system with GNSS- based autonomous perception are investigated and demonstrated in the practical environment, which illustrate the necessity and advantages of the utilization of GNSS, especially BDS (BeiDou Navigation Satellite System), in future safety-related railway systems in China. 2. Multi-sensor data fusion In existing railway signaling systems, the track occupancy of the train is determined by the track circuit, and the exact along-track lo- cation of the train is calculated by integrating both the train-borne odometer (or Doppler radar) and track-side Balises. The novel train- centric signaling system determines the track occupancy and train lo- cation without relying on those track-side facilities. Thus, raw mea- surements from multiple train-borne sensors will be integrated to achieve the required accuracy level, reduce the uncertainty and en- hance the dependability. 2.1. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Zero on-site testing on GNSS for train control towards digital railways
content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 116–124 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article , Wenzheng Qiaoa, Baigen Caia,b,c, Jian Wanga,b,c, Jiang Liua,b,c, Yuchen Zhaoa, Zero on-site testing on GNSS for train control towards digital railways Debiao Lua,b,c,⁎ Chunjie Qiaod, Dirk Spiegele a School of Automation and Intelligence, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c Beijing Engineering Center for EMC and GNSS Technologies, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China d Hunan Matrix Electronic Technology Co., Ltd., Changsha 410205, China e DB InfraGO AG, Berlin 10557, Germany ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: GNSS testing Railway train localization 3D environment modelling Digital track map Zero on-site testing The integration of Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) technology into railway train control systems is a crucial step toward achieving the vision of a digital railway. Traditional train control systems undergo extensive in-house tests and prolonged field tests for certification and approval before operational deployment, leading to high costs, delays, and operational disruptions. This paper introduces a GNSS-based train control localization framework which eliminates the need for on-site testing by leveraging train movement dynamics and 3D en- vironment modeling to create a zero on-site testing platform. The proposed framework simulates train move- ment and the surrounding 3D environment using collected railway line location data and environmental attri- butes to generate realistic multipath signals and obscuration effects. This approach enables comprehensive laboratory-based case studies for train localization, reducing the huge amount test of needed for physical field trials. The framework is established in house, using the data collected at the Test Base of China Academy of Railway Sciences (Circular Railway). Results from the open area and cutting environment tests demonstrate high localization accuracy repeatability within the simulated environment, validating the feasibility and effectiveness of zero on-site testing for GNSS-based train control systems. This research highlights the potential of GNSS simulation platforms in enhancing cost efficiency, operational safety, and accuracy for future digital railways.
